narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to pursue full-time studies, you've put real effort into your law school application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted a 65.1% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which unfortunately tipped the scales toward rejecting your application to help ensure we select students most likely to succeed long-term. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic performance in school, especially your lower rankings—landing in the 2nd decile for Year 3 grades and 3rd decile for Year 1, both well below the typical student's mid-range standing—which stood out as the strongest factors pushing the model toward forecasting a bar failure. Your LSAT score of 30, undergraduate GPA of 2.80, and full-time enrollment intentions each pulled in the opposite direction, suggesting potential for passing, but they weren't strong enough to outweigh those early academic decile concerns. Compared to average applicants, who typically rank around the 5th or 6th decile in those years with higher LSATs near 37 and GPAs around 3.2, your profile appeared riskier in the model's eyes despite your full-time commitment aligning with the vast majority of successful students. Your family income in the middle bracket had a minor nudge toward the failure prediction but wasn't a major player here. I know this rejection stings, especially when you've worked hard to get this far as a dedicated guy from a white, middle-class background navigating competitive paths. The good news is this isn't the end—consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average levels, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework if possible, or targeting schools with more holistic reviews that value your full-time drive. You might also reflect on study habits from those early school years to build a stronger narrative for future apps. Many students in similar spots turn things around with targeted improvements, and with your determination, you're well-positioned to do just that—keep pushing forward.
